beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. Give Claude a request — create a poster for a jazz festival. What comes back is not a document. It is a two-act performance. First, Claude writes a design philosophy — a manifesto for an art movement. Second, Claude uses that philosophy to generate a PDF or PNG canvas. Ninety percent visual. Ten percent text. The philosophy is the instruction set. Let me show you how it works.

props.command: Create a poster for a jazz festival. Use the canvas-design skill.
output: Movement: "Geometric Silence" — grid precision, negative space, restrained type
Step 2: Canvas → poster.pdf — 90% visual, 10% essential text

beats.act: Act 1 — what the skill is / anatomy
beats.narration_text: Canvas-design is a two-step creative pipeline in one SKILL.md. Step one: design philosophy. Claude names an aesthetic movement — Geometric Silence, Chromatic Language — and writes four to six paragraphs on how that movement manifests as form, space, color, and composition. Step two: canvas. Claude reads the philosophy and generates a single-page PDF or PNG that is ninety percent visual design. Text is accent only. The philosophy is the brief.

The pipeline is linear. A user request comes in — poster, piece of art, any static visual. Claude runs step one and outputs a dot-md design philosophy file. That philosophy gets read in step two, where Claude generates the canvas — dot-pdf or dot-png — targeting museum and magazine quality. No loops. No revision cycle. The SKILL.md even says: treat the first page as just a single page in a coffee table book waiting to be filled.

This is what a canvas-design philosophy looks like — rendered from the SKILL.md examples. The movement is named in one to two words. The philosophy articulates five visual dimensions: space and form, color and material, scale and rhythm, composition and balance, visual hierarchy. The most striking instruction: the word craftsmanship appears multiple times — the SKILL.md says to REPEATEDLY emphasize that the final work appears as though it took countless hours to create. That is a design constraint built into the skill itself.

Here is the canvas phase rendered live from the Geometric Silence philosophy in SKILL.md. Grid-based precision. Bold negative space. Minimal typography — small essential text, large quiet zones. This is what the SKILL.md calls Swiss formalism meets Brutalist material honesty. The skill instructs Claude to: use repeating patterns, add sparse clinical typography, treat the composition as if it were a diagram from an imaginary discipline. The visual language is systematic, not decorative.

beats.act: Act 4 — the design tell / Teardown moment
beats.narration_text: Here is the Teardown moment. This skill has the largest creative surface of any skill in this batch — the instruction literally says embrace ultimate design freedom and choice. But look at the constraint underneath. The SKILL.md pre-approves the human's critique. The FINAL STEP opens with: The user ALREADY said it isn't perfect enough. That line is baked in. It is not a response to feedback — it is pre-emptive refinement. What it gets right: Claude writes the design brief itself — never guessing the aesthetic. What it bites: canvas quality depends entirely on Claude's code generation ability. A complex composition can produce overlapping elements. The pre-baked critique runs regardless of quality.

Canvas-design is a two-act pipeline. Act one: Claude writes the brief. Act two: Claude executes it. The philosophy makes the art intentional, not generated. The pre-baked make-it-pristine instruction is the most interesting design choice — the skill critiques itself before the human can.

artifactLines: Two-act pipeline: Claude writes the brief (philosophy.md), then executes it (canvas)
Philosophy names the movement — form, space, color, composition, hierarchy
Craftsmanship mandate is structural: REPEATEDLY emphasized in SKILL.md
Pre-baked critique: FINAL STEP runs before the human says anything
Limit: canvas quality depends on Claude's code generation — no layout guard

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: I have a concept for a poster — a meditation retreat in the mountains. Use the canvas-design skill. Before you write any code, show me the design philosophy — the movement name and its five visual dimensions — and tell me what the conceptual soul of the piece will be. That phrase — the conceptual soul — comes directly from the SKILL.md. Asking for it before the code forces Claude to commit to the aesthetic brief out loud, so you can redirect it before any pixels are generated.

props.command: I have a concept for a poster — a meditation retreat in the mountains. Use the canvas-design skill. Before you write any code, show me the design philosophy — the movement name and its five visual dimensions — and tell me what the conceptual soul of the piece will be.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Canvas. Liam, in for Bear.
